title: Symplectic Rigidity of Anosov Flows Under Orbit Equivalence
speaker: Thomas Massoni
abstract:
A classical construction of Mitsumatsu, later generalized by Hozoori, associates to any (oriented) Anosov flow on a closed 3-manifold M a Liouville structure on the thickening [−1,1]×M. This construction also extends to suitable taut foliations. 
From the dynamical viewpoint, it is natural to study Anosov flows up to orbit equivalence, i.e., homeomorphisms sending (unparametrized) orbits to orbits. Such maps are typically not smooth, so the effect on the associated Liouville structures is far from obvious. In this talk, I will present joint work with Jonathan Bowden showing that orbit equivalent Anosov flows induce exact symplectomorphic Liouville structures. A similar result holds for taut foliations. Consequently, the symplectic invariants (Floer homology, Fukaya category, etc.) attached to Anosov flows are preserved under orbit equivalence.

proof_stages.label: Anosov-flow data and defining one-forms
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the three-dimensional Anosov setting, records the stable/unstable splitting, weak foliations, orientability, regularity, tautness and adapted metrics, and dualizes this data into defining one-forms with signed expansion rates.

proof_stages.label: Mitsumatsu-Hozoori contact and Liouville construction
proof_stages.summary: From the defining pair alpha_u, alpha_s one builds positive and negative contact structures and interpolates their contact forms across [-1,1] x M to obtain the associated Liouville structure, with Reeb-transversality and skeleton features noted.

proof_stages.label: Orbit equivalence and weak-foliation functoriality
proof_stages.summary: An oriented orbit equivalence is a generally nonsmooth homeomorphism preserving oriented flow orbits, and the topological characterization of asymptotic behavior forces it to send weak stable and weak unstable foliations to their counterparts.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity statements, invariants, and converse questions
proof_stages.summary: The main theorems state that orbit-equivalent oriented Anosov flows have exact-symplectomorphic associated Liouville structures and deformation-equivalent biconcontact structures, motivating invariant consequences and converse questions related to skeleton dynamics and finiteness of Anosov flows.

proof_stages.label: Controlled smoothing of orbit equivalences
proof_stages.summary: The first proof step replaces the orbit-equivalence homeomorphism by a smooth diffeomorphism that is C0-close to it and sends the source weak stable and unstable plane fields arbitrarily close to the target ones.

proof_stages.label: Transverse uniqueness of contact approximations
proof_stages.summary: A strengthened Vogel-type uniqueness theorem is established for contact approximations of the C1 weak foliation, keeping the homotopy of contact structures transverse to a fixed smooth transverse vector field.

proof_stages.label: Assembly into Liouville and biconcontact rigidity
proof_stages.summary: The controlled smoothing pushes the contact pair close to the target foliation, transverse uniqueness supplies the needed contact homotopies, and an exact leafwise two-form plus a final upgrade from pre-Liouville data gives the claimed exact symplectomorphism and deformation equivalence.

examples.name: Geodesic flow on a hyperbolic surface
examples.note: The unit tangent bundle geodesic flow of a hyperbolic surface is used as the standard concrete Anosov flow motivating the definitions and the later algebraic description via PSL(2,R).
examples.engagement_reason: The geodesic flow on a hyperbolic surface is given as a prototypical Anosov flow and algebraic model, but no computation or property is worked out for it.

examples.name: Transverse saddle and bisector-twisting picture
examples.note: The local stable/unstable saddle picture shows why a bisector plane field containing the flow twists under the dynamics, making the contact condition visually plausible before the formula is given.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws and reasons through the transverse saddle picture, following how bisectors are pushed and twist under the flow to produce contact planes.

examples.name: Closed-orbit cylinders as exact Lagrangians
examples.note: In response to a question about invariants, the speaker notes that a closed Anosov orbit times the interval gives an exact Lagrangian, illustrating possible Fukaya-categorical information and a possible converse strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the construction to closed orbits by taking a closed orbit times the interval to obtain exact Lagrangian cylinders and discusses their role in the Fukaya category/skeleton picture.

examples.name: Two-dimensional local model for smoothing a foliated homeomorphism
examples.note: The map h(x,y)=(a(x,y),b(y)) between horizontal foliations is a toy model explaining why local monotone smoothing is easy while the global inductive interpolation is delicate.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives a two-dimensional local model for a foliated homeomorphism, writes its form as h(x,y)=(a(x,y),b(y)), states monotonicity conditions, and explains the smoothing approximation.
